topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash. Elias stared at the chipped Formica table, the silence in the café amplifying the low thrum of his own irritation. He’d spent the last hour replaying a single, innocuous anecdote about a summer vacation, trying to find the moment he’d been forgotten. When Liam finally slid into the booth across from him, all easy laughter and familiar slouch, Elias just nodded curtly, taking a slow, deliberate sip of his drink. "So," Liam began, leaning forward with an eager smile, "remember that time we tried to build that ridiculous fort in the woods behind your house?" Elias felt a tightening in his chest, a familiar knot of resentment. He just stared at the condensation tracing paths down his glass. "I... I don't recall," Elias managed, his voice flat, devoid of any warmth. Liam blinked, the smile faltering slightly, a flicker of confusion crossing his features before he quickly smoothed it over. "Oh. Right. Well, that sounds like a good story." Elias pushed his untouched pastry away. The whole interaction felt like an unnecessary imposition on his already frayed nerves.

[story 2]

Maya scowled, crossing her arms tightly over her chest, the fabric of her worn sweater feeling like a physical constraint. She watched Liam talk animatedly about his weekend plans, his gestures broad and open, and a deep sense of isolation settled over her. She had rehearsed this conversation a hundred times in her head, but the reality of it was far more abrasive. "You know," Maya started, her tone clipped, "I was thinking about that camping trip last summer. The one near the lake. Remember how we got completely lost trying to find the waterfall?" Liam paused, his hand halfway to his mouth. His expression shifted, a subtle widening of his eyes that suggested he was searching through a distant, hazy archive. "Hmm? I... I don't think so," he said, a slight hesitation in his voice. Maya felt a sharp, unpleasant sensation rising in her throat. She wanted to snap something, to demand an acknowledgment, but instead, she just let her gaze drop to the tabletop, focusing intently on a tiny scratch in the wood grain, wishing the floor would just open up and swallow her whole.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an oppressive monotony that matched the mood inside Clara. She tapped her pen against her notebook rhythmically, a sharp, impatient sound in the quiet space. Liam approached her desk, radiating an almost blinding level of enthusiasm. "Hey, Clara! I was just thinking about that awful movie we saw in middle school. The one with the terrible special effects?" He leaned in, his voice bright and conversational. Clara didn't look up immediately. When she finally did, her expression was carefully neutral, bordering on stony. "I'm not sure I know what you're referencing," she stated, her tone measured and cool. Liam’s enthusiasm deflated instantly, replaced by a noticeable slackening of his posture. He shifted his weight awkwardly. "Oh. Right. Well, maybe we can talk about something else?" Clara picked up her textbook, deliberately turning her attention back to the dense text, feeling a distinct, heavy weight settle on her shoulders. The air around her felt thick, unyielding.
